title: Ancient Mesopotamia
description: Practice your skills with a quiz on ancient Mesopotamia!

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**This mosaic can be used as evidence of which of the following in ancient Mesopotamia?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The Standard of Ur, from Mesopotamia c. 2600 BCE. This art depicts food, such as fish and grain, being carried by farmers and fisherman (or soldiers carrying war booty), seated dignitaries, a lyre instrument player, a singer, and the king.
options.caption: The Standard of Ur, from Mesopotamia c. 2600 BCE. This art depicts food, such as fish and grain, being carried by farmers and fisherman (or soldiers carrying war booty), seated dignitaries, a lyre instrument player, a singer, and the king. Image credit: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_of_Ur#/media/File:Standard_of_Ur_-_War.jpg)

choices.content: Valuing egalitarianism over of social hierarchy
Increasing specialization of labor
Artistic representations of legal codes
Ziggurats and monumental architecture in art

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]


**This sculpture is best seen as evidence of which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
"Warka Vase" from the Sumerian city Urik (modern Warka), Iraq, ca. 3200-3000 BCE.

This vase shows a religious festival for the goddess Inanna.  The lowest band on the vase shows ewes and rams above crops and a wavy line representing water, indicating that Inanna had blessed Uruk's inhabitants with good crops and increased herds.
options.caption:
"Warka Vase" from the Sumerian city Urik (modern Warka), Iraq, ca. 3200-3000 BCE.

This vase shows a religious festival for the goddess Inanna.  The lowest band on the vase shows ewes and rams above crops and a wavy line representing water, indicating that Inanna had blessed Uruk's inhabitants with good crops and increased herds.

Image credit: [University of Chicago](https://oi-archive.uchicago.edu/OI/IRAQ/dbfiles/objects/14.htm)

choices.content: Sumerians recognized the power of the environment to greatly impact their lives
Sumerians believed in deities that which were disinterested in human affairs
Sumerians used religion to establish social hierarchies
Sumerians celebrated the importance of specialization of labor in daily routines

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"If a man gives his wife a field, garden, and house with a deed – and if then after the death of her husband the sons make no claim – then the mother may bequeath all her assets to the one of her sons whom she prefers, and  leave nothing to his brothers.  
>If this woman does not wish to remain in her husband's house, then he shall compensate her for the dowry that she brought with her from her father's house, and she may go.
>A man may take a wife, but she bears him no children, and he intends to take another wife. If he takes this second wife, and brings her into the house, the second wife shall not be equal to his first wife."

From _The Code of Hammurabi_

**A historian would most likely use this passage as evidence for which of the following claims about Ancient Mesopotamia?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women’s legal status and protections were elevated such that they were equal to men’s legal status
Women did have legal status and protections, but they were unequal to those of men
Women had no legal status or protections, and were dependent upon the status of their male relatives
Women’s legal status and protections varied based on age and number of sons.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"If a man gives his wife a field, garden, and house with a deed – and if then after the death of her husband the sons make no claim – then the wife may bequeath all her assets to the one of her sons whom she prefers, and leave nothing to his brothers.  
>If a woman does not wish to remain in her husband's house, then he shall compensate her for the dowry that she brought with her from her father's house, and she may go.

>A man may take a wife, but she bears him no children, and so he intends to take another wife. If he takes this second wife, and brings her into the house, the second wife shall not be equal to his first wife."


From _The Code of Hammurabi_

**How do the laws in this excerpt compare to gender roles prior to the emergence of civilization?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They are more defined and regimented than earlier gender roles
They are less defined and regimented than earlier gender roles
Earlier groups did not differentiate between the genders in any meaningful way
Earlier groups placed greater restrictions on women’s legal status

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"That which might be the first clear representation of a horse in the Middle East can be found in a tablet form Ur III, reign of Šu-Sin, 2037 – 2029 BCE . . . Therefore, it is possible to place domestic horses in Ur prior to 2000 BCE. Since horse domestication took place in the steppes of northern Kazakhstan, a link between this region and the Middle East must be found, in order to account for the horses' presence there. Only after c. 2100 BCE did horses begin to appear regularly in the Near and Middle east. This means that sometime between c.2500 and c.2100 BCE, there was an increase in the influx of horses into the region."

From _The Origin and the Spread of the War Chariot_ by Elias Manuel Morgado Pinheiro (2010).

**The author's argument could best be understood in the context of which of the following historical developments?**




[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There was an exchange of technology and methods of transportation between the steppes and Mesopotamia.
The independent development of new technologies within Mesopotamian society spread to the steppes
Mesopotamian military expeditions spread their civilization and city-states into the Eurasian steppes
There was an exchange of technology and methods of transportation between the Mongol Empire and the steppes.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 2]]

**The object in the image best illustrates which of the following cultural processes that occurred before 600 BCE?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.caption: Image of Ziggurat at Ur in Mesopotamia. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ziggurat_of_Ur#/media/File:Ancient_ziggurat_at_Ali_Air_Base_Iraq_2005.jpg)

choices.content: The codification of laws in early structures built for legal governance
The development of monumental architecture for religious purposes
The use of urban planning to maintain and store a stable food supply
The construction of buildings to produce and store written records

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

> "How lowly is the poor man! 

> The small area around the oven is an entire mill house to him.

> His ripped garment will not be mended.

> What he has lost will not be sought for!"

From an Ancient Sumerian proverb

**Which of the following cultural developments does this proverb best represent?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The use of writing for record keeping
Writing for artistic and literary purposes
The use of writing for legal codification
The use of writing for religious codification

assessment_items.question_content:
Refer to the image below. 

[[☃ image 1]]


**This artifact is best seen as evidence for which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Inscribed clay tablet from Mesopotamia, c. 2052 B.C.E.
options.caption:
Inscribed clay tablet from Mesopotamia, c. 2052 B.C.E.

Image credit: [LACMA](https://collections.lacma.org/node/228141)

choices.content: The advent of writing using the Indus script
The advent of writing using cuneiform
The advent of law and political authority
The development of trade

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

> "How lowly is the poor man! 

> The small area around the oven is an entire mill house to him.

> His ripped garment will not be mended.

> What he has lost will not be sought for!"

From an Ancient Sumerian proverb

**This proverb would have originally been written in which of the following scripts?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Hieroglyphics
Cuneiform
Aramaic
Hebrew

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"If any one strike the body of a man higher in rank than he, he shall receive sixty blows with an ox-whip in public.

>  If a free-born man strike the body of another free-born man or equal rank, he shall pay one gold mina."

From _The Code of Hammurabi_ 

**How did this excerpt from the _Code of Hammurabi_ reflect the social hierarchy of ancient Babylonia?**



[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Punishments were adjusted depending on the status of the perpetrator and the victim
The laws ensured an egalitarian society where all punishments were never more than 60 blows
Laws and punishments only applied to slaves and common people, not to elites
Punishments were based solely on the severity of the crime committed and equivalent gold fine

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"If a judge tries a case, reaches a decision, and presents his judgment in writing, and if an error later appears in his decision that is through his own fault, then he shall pay twelve times the fine set by him in the case. He shall also be publicly removed from the judge's bench, and never again sit there to render judgement."

From _The Code of Hammurabi_

**What does this excerpt tell us about the legal structure of Babylonia?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Judges had the power to remove themselves from their jobs if they felt unfit to serve
The state’s legal system had built-in ways of eliminating people who caused an obstruction of justice
The state employed a large number of people to determine fairness in completed legal cases
Most judges were wealthy enough to pay a fine up to twelve times higher than fines they would assign

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"How lowly is the poor man! 

> A mill house is (for him) the edge of the oven.

> His ripped garment will not be mended.

> What he has lost will not be sought for!"

From an Ancient Sumerian proverb

**The content of this proverb could best be used as evidence of what development in the ancient world?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Social hierarchies intensified
Specialization of labor decreased
Religious belief systems became more codified
Environmental conditions limited food production
